title: Long-Running Sessions: Keeping AI Work Coherent Over Time
thesis: Long-running AI sessions need clear goals, summaries, memory, checkpoints, context pruning, and stopping rules to avoid drift and wasted work.

claims.claim: A long-running session is useful only when the system can remember what matters, recognize progress, and decide when to stop.

evidence.snippet: Generative agents use a memory stream, retrieval, and reflection to maintain coherent behavior over extended simulated time.

counterevidence.summary: Memory and summarization are selective. Important details can be compressed out or retrieved incorrectly, so remembering what matters is not guaranteed.

claims.claim: Keeping extended work coherent is already familiar from workflow orchestration, durable execution, project management, and process control.

evidence.snippet: Reasoning and acting have a long history in AI; ReAct explicitly connects the loop of thought, action, and observation to prior work in reinforcement learning and decision making.

counterevidence.summary: Older fields usually assume formally defined goals, stable environments, or fixed stages. LLM-based sessions face ambiguous, shifting goals and improvised steps.

claims.claim: In practice, long-running sessions combine summarization, checkpoints, context pruning, and prompt caching to keep the active window focused without losing the goal.

evidence.snippet: Self-Refine iteratively refines outputs using self-generated feedback, showing how a loop of generation and summary can extend a single session.
ReAct interleaves reasoning traces and actions, relying on the session history to stay focused on the task while observing new information.
Prompt caching reduces the cost and latency of repeated context in multi-turn sessions, making it more practical to keep long-running sessions coherent.

counterevidence.summary: Not every useful task needs a long session; some problems are best solved by a single, carefully crafted prompt or a stateless function.

claims.claim: Without summaries, checkpoints, and stopping rules, long-running sessions drift, waste resources, or resume in broken states.

evidence.snippet: Reflexion shows that language agents can benefit from verbal reinforcement signals to correct trajectory, implying that unguided trajectories can drift or repeat errors.
Long-term memory retrieval in generative agents can fail to surface the most relevant context, leading to behavior that diverges from prior decisions.

counterevidence.summary: Simple exit conditions such as cost or time budgets can prevent runaway work, but they do not guarantee the original goal has been reached.

claims.claim: Prompt caching can cut the cost and latency of repeated context in long sessions, making extended sessions more practical.

evidence.snippet: A cross-provider evaluation of multi-turn research agents with 10,000-token system prompts found prompt caching reduced API costs by 41–80% and improved time-to-first-token by 13–31%.

counterevidence.summary: Caching benefits depend on context size, provider implementation, and how much of the prompt is repeated; small or highly varied sessions may see little gain.

sources.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
Cross-Provider Evaluation of Prompt Caching in Multi-Turn Research Agents

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit.
Treat maturity=seed as an explicit uncertainty marker.
Do not present long-running AI sessions as capable of remembering everything or running unattended indefinitely.
When summarizing, preserve the plain-language-first, technical-depth-later structure.

reviews.notes: Sibling-agent review against article structure. Privacy scan passed. No proprietary or personal content detected.
Human author approved the draft for publication.
